title: 第二講
en: DEFINITION OF RELIGION. Definition of Religion, why wanted.

IF the Science of Religion is to be treated as one of the natural sciences, it is clear that we must begin with a careful collection of facts, illustrating the origin, the growth, and the decay of religion.

But we shall find it impossible to do so, unless we first enter on a preliminary and, I must add, a somewhat difficult inquiry, namely, What is meant by religion. Unless we can come to a clear understanding on that point, we shall find it impossible to determine what facts to include, and what facts to exclude in collecting our evidence for the study of religion.

What then is religion ? To many people this will sound a very easy question, as easy as the question, What is man ? Practical people object to such questions, and consider any attempt to answer them as mere waste of time. Now it is quite true that there is a kind of public opinion, which for all ordinary purposes settles the meaning of words, and by which we may allow ourselves to be guided in the daily concerns of life. But in philosophical discussions this is strictly forbidden. What is philosophy but a perpetual criticism and correction of language, and the history of philosophy but a succession of new definitions assigned to old and familiar terms ?
Great differences in defining* Religion.

Besides, there is anything but agreement on the true meaning of religion. Most people, whatever their opinions might be on other points, would probably hold that religion must always have something to do with God or the gods. But even that is not the case. Buddhism, for instance, which is a creed professed by the largest number of human beings, recognises, as taught by Buddha &akyamuni, no god, or at all events no creator of the universe, and it has been held in consequence that Buddhism could not be called religion.
Is Buddhism a Religion?

Now it is quite true, we may so define religion that the name could not be applied to Buddhism ; but the question is, who has the right so to narrow the definition of the word ' religion ' that it should cease to be applicable to the creed of the majority of mankind? You see that the right of definition is a most sacred right, and has to be carefully guarded, if we wish to avoid the danger of mere logomachies. How often have I been asked, Do you call Buddha's religion a religion, do you call DarwinV philosophy philosophy, or Wagner's music music ? What can we answer under such provocation, except, Define what you mean by religion, define what you mean by philosophy, define what you mean by music, and then, and then only, we may possibly come to an agreement as to whether Buddha's doctrines may be called religion, Darwin's writings philosophy, and Wagner's compositions music. I know full well that nothing irritates an adversary so much as to be asked for a definition ; and yet it is well known, or ought to be well known, that defini- DEFINITION OF RELIGION. ^9 tion formed the very 'foundation of the philosophy of the ancients, of Socrates, Plato and Aristotle, while the absence of proper definitions has been and is still the curse of modern philosophy1.
Definition of Definition.
But before we can give a definition of religion, we must first give a definition of definition itself, however pedantic such a request may appear.
There are at least three kinds of definitions, the etymological, the historical, and the dogmatic.
Etymological Definition.

Many people still imagine that an etymology is in itself a definition. This was an impression which prevailed widely in early times 2, before the true principles of etymology had been discovered ; and it prevails even now, though there is no longer any excuse for it. Homer, for instance, is very fond of etymologies which are to account for the peculiar character of certain gods and heroes. Plato extends this practice even more widely, though he often leaves us in doubt whether he is really serious in his etymologies or not. You know how in his Cratylus (410) he derives a?/p, air, from aiptiv, to raise, as the element which raises things from the earth ; How he explains aldrjp, ether, as aecOeijp, because this element is always running in a flux about the air (ael del aepa pecov). He derives deoi, the gods, also from the same root delv, to run, because he suspected, as he says 3, ' that the sun, moon, earth, stars, and heaven, which are still the 1 See Mill, Three Essays on Religion, p. 4.
2 Cf. Sarikhyatattvakaiimudi, 4 ; tannirvafcanaw fea lakshanam, 'the etymological interpretation is the definition.'
3 Cratylus, 397 C.

gods of many barbarians, were the only gods known to the aboriginal Hellenes ; and seeing that they were always moving and running, from this their running nature, they called them gods or runners ; and afterwards,, when they had discovered all the other gods, they retained the old name.' Aristotle was more sparing in his etymological definitions, yet he too derived aldrjp, the ether, from ael Oelv, because it was always running and moving1.

The Komans followed the example of the Greeks 2. Poets like Lucretius and Ovid indulged in etymologies, whenever they seemed to agree with their opinions, and to the latest times Koman lawyers delighted in supporting their definitions of legal terms by more or less fanciful derivations.

In India also these etymological definitions were recognised from the earliest times. They are generally introduced in the following way : ' This is the saddlehood of a saddle that we sit on it ' ; ' this is the roadhood of a road that we ride 3 on it ' ; ' this is the heaven-hood of heaven that it has been heaved on high.' Only, while these etymologies are historically correct, any etymology is welcome to the authors of the Brahma^a or the Nirukta. if only it explains some meaning of the word.

In some cases these etymological definitions are very useful, but they require the greatest caution. First of all, many popular etymologies 4 are phonetically untenable and historically wrong. God, for in- 1 T)e Mundo, ed. Didot, vol. iii. p. 628. 1. 28 ; Sta to del 6eii>.
2 Lersch, Die Sprachphilosopliie der Alten, vol. iii ; Cic. Nat, Deor. iii. 24.
3 See Academy, Dec. 1888 ; also Plutarch, Fragm. 21. 27.
4 Varro. LI. v. 7. ed. Egger. Quattuor explicandi gradus : infimus is quo etiam populus venit. Quis enim non videt unde arenifodinae et viocurus?' Lersch, 1. c. vol. iii. p. 126.

DEFINITION OF RELIGION. 31 stance, cannot be derived from good, because phonetic laws will not allow it, and because the two words run parallel, and never approach one another, as far as we can follow their history.

But even where an etymology is unassailable on phonetic and historical grounds, it can never give us more than the first starting-point of a word. It may teach us how the object to be named was first conceived, but no more. We know, for instance, that deus in Latin represents the Sanskrit (leva, perhaps also the Greek 6e6s, though neither of these etymologies is in strict accordance with phonetic rules1, and that deva meant originally, bright. This is extremely important as showing us that one of the many conceptions of the Divine started from the concept of bright and beneficent beings, such as sun, and moon, and stars, in opposition to the dark and deadly aspects of the night; but to imagine that this could help us to understand the concept of God in the mind of such a thinker as Pascal, would be absurd. We can never be too grateful, if we can discover the germinal idea of a word, if we can prove, for instance, that dens was originally no more than a bright being, that a priest was originally an elder, a minister a servant, a bishop an overseer ; but if we were to give these etymologies as more than historical curiosities, and mistake them for definitions, we should only prove our ignorance of the nature of language, which is in a constant state of ebb and flow, and exhibits to us the process of continuous evolution better than any other part of nature.
1 See Selected Essays, i. p. 215. I still hold to the opinions there expressed.
Historical Definition.

We now come to historical definitions. What I call an historical definition is an account of these very changes which take place in the meaning of a word, so long as it is left to the silent and unconscious influences which proceed from the vast community of the speakers of one and the same language. Thus an historical definition of deus would have to show the various changes which led from cleva, bright, as applied to the sun, the dawn and other heavenly phenomena, to the Devas, as powers within or behind these heavenly bodies, and lastly to the beneficent agents in nature or above nature, whom the Hindus called Devas, and the Romans dii. As the biography of a man may be called his best definition, what I call biographies of words are perhaps the most useful definitions which it is in our power to give.
Dogmatic Definition.

Lastly come the dogmatic definitions, by which I mean definitions given on the authority of individuals, who, whatever a word may have meant etymologically, and whatever it may have come to mean historically, declare that, for their own purposes, they intend to use it in such and such a sense. This is chiefly done by philosophers, lawyers, and men of science, who feel unable to use important words with all the vagueness of their etymological and historical meaning, and determine once for all, generally by the old logical method of settling their genus and their specific difference, in what exact sense they ought to be employed in future.
Let us now see how these three kinds of definition DEFINITION OF RELIGION. 33 have been applied to the word with which we have to deal, namely religion.
Etymological Definition of Religio.

The etymological definition of religion has attracted considerable interest among theologians, owing to that kind of tacit persuasion that the etymology of the word must somehow or other help to disclose its real meaning. It is well known that Lactantius derived religio from religare, to bind or hold back, and he did so, not simply as a philologist, but as a theologian. ' We are born,' he says, ' under the condition that, when born, we should offer to God our justly due services, should know Him only, and follow Him only. We are tied to God and bound to Him (religati) by the bond of piety, and from this has religion itself received its name, and not, as Cicero has interpreted it, from attention (a relegehdo)1.'

Before we examine this etymology, it will be useful to give the etymology which Lactantius ascribes to Cicero, and which he is bold enough to reject. Cicero says : ' Those who carefully took in hand all things pertaining to the worship of the gods, were called religiosi, from reUgere, — as neat people (elegantes) were so called from elegere2, to pick out; likewise diligent people, diligentes, from diligere, to choose, to value, and intelligent people from Intel- 1 Lactantius, Instttut. Div. iv. 28, 'Hac conditione gignimur, ut generati nos Deo justa et debita obsequia praebeamus, hunc solum noverimus, hunc sequamur. Hoc vinculo pietatis obstricti Deo et religati sumua ; unde ipsa religio nomen accepit, non, ut Cicero interpretatus est, a relegendo.'
2 Rather from a lost verb elegare.
ligere, to understand ; for in all these words there is the meaning of legere, to gather, to choose, the same as in rellgiobus 1.

Let us first clear the ground of some statements which are repeated again and again, but which have really no foundation. It is often said that Varro2 supports the etymology of Lactantius, but Varro simply treats of legere and legio, and thus supports indirectly the etymology of Cicero, rather than that of Lactantius.

Festus, again, if he is to be quoted at all as having given an etymology of rellgio, sides with Cicero, and not with Lactantius, for he says that people are called religiosi if they make a choice (delectus) of what has to be done or to be omitted in the worship of the gods, according to the custom of the state, and do not entangle themselves in superstitions 3.

Of later writers St. Augustin follows sometimes the one, sometimes the other derivation, as it suits his purpose ; while among modern theologians it has actually been maintained that rcligio was descended from vellgare as well as from relegere, so as to combine the meanings of both 4.

His enim in verbis omnibus inest vis legendi eadem quae in religiose'
2 Varro, Be ling. lat. v. 68 ; ed. Egger. Legio, ' quod leguntur milites in delectu ;' Nitzsch, Studien mid Kritikcn, i. p. 527.
3 Festus, p. 236, 'Religiosi dicuntur, qui faciendarum praetermittendarumque rerum divinarum secundum morem civitatis delectum habent nee se superstitionibus implicant.'

DEFINITION OP RELIGION. 35 be denied that religio might have sprung from religa re quite as well as from rdegere. The ordinary objection that from religare we should have religatio, and not religio, has no real weight, for we find by the side of opinari such words as opinio, not opinatio, and necopinus ; and by the side of rebellare, rebellis and rebellio. In lictor also, if it meant originally a man who binds the criminal, we should have to admit a root ligere, by the side of ligare.

The real objection to our deriving religio from religare is the fact that in classical Latin religare is never used in the sense of binding or holding back. In that sense we should have expected obligatio, or possibly obligio, but not religio. Cicero's etymology is therefore decidedly preferable, as more in accordance with Latin idiom. Relegere would be the opposite of neglegere or negligere1, and as neglegere meant 'not to care,' relegere would naturally have meant 'to care,' 'to regard,' ' to revere'2. From a verse quoted by Nigidius Figulus from an ancient writer, and preserved by Gellius (iv. 9), we learn that religeus was actually used, as opposed to religiosus. He said: Religentem esse oportet, religiosus ne fuas, 'it is right to be reverent, but do not be religious,' that is, superstitious 3.

Quocirca religiosus is appellabatur qui nimia et superstitiosa rehgione sese alligaverat, eaque res vitio assignabatur.' -bed D 2 36 LECTURE II.

The German word Anclacht, literally thoughtfulness, then reverence, has sometimes been compared with religio, but there is a slight difference, for Andacht conveys the meaning of meditation rather than of regard and reverence.

There is one more etymological definition of religion which Gellius (iv. 9) ascribes to one Masurius Sabinus. He derived religiosum, in the sense of sacred, from relinquere, to leave or put aside, as something too sacred for ordinary purposes1. As phonetic laws would not allow of this derivation, we need not discuss it further.
So much for the etymology of religio, which in its first conception can only have meant respect, care, reverence.
Historical Definition of Religio.

We now come to what I called the historical definition, or what others might prefer to call an historical description of the fates of the word religio, while confined to its own native soil. Most words, particularly those which form the subject of controversies, have had a history of their own. Their meaning has changed from century to century, often from generation to generation ; nay, like the expression of the human face, the expression of a word also may change from moment to moment. In one sense our historical definition may be called the biography of praeter ista,' thus Gellius continues, -quae Nigidius elicit, alio quodam diverticulo significations, religiosus pro casto atque observant! cohibentique sese certis legibus finibusque dici coeptus.'

1 'Masurius autem Sabinus in commentariis quos de indigenia composuit, religiosum, inquit, est quod propter sanctitatem aliquam remotum ac sepositum a nobis est, verbum a relinquendo dictum, tamquam caerimonia a carendo.' Gellius, ed. Hertz, iv. 9.

DEFINITION OP RELIGION. 37 a word, and if only it can be recovered with any approach to completeness, such a biography conveys to us more information than can be gathered from any logical or etymological definition.

So long as the word religio remains on Roman soil, all changes of meaning seem perfectly intelligible, if only we take into account the influence of those forces which determine the growth of meaning in all words. Afterwards, when the word religio is transferred from a Roman to a Christian atmosphere, from classical to mediaeval Latin and the modern Romanic dialects, from popular parlance to technical theology, the case becomes different. We then enter on purely dogmatic or self-willed definitions, the natural growth of language seems arrested, and all we can do is to register the various meanings which have been assigned to the word religion by philosophers and theologians of authority and influence.

Tracing the history of religio, we find it used in Latin in its original and wider sense of regard or respect, in such expressions as religio jurisjurandi, reverence for an oath, as distinguished from metus deorum, fear of the gods1.

Religio and metus occur frequently together, for instance, Cic. ii.inVerr. 4, 45, 101, ut earn (cupiditatem) non metus, non religio contineret, where we can translate the two words metus and religio by fear and awe, fear expressing the fear of men or of consequences, awe the fear of the gods It is said in another place that when the moon was suddenly eclipsed on a 1 Cic. Font. ix. 30. 'An vero istas nationes religione jurisjurandi ac metu deorum immortalium in testimoniis dicendis commoveri arbitramini, quae tantum a ceterarum gentium more ac natura dissentiunt,'

clear night, the whole army was perturbed religione et metu, by awe and fear. Such expressions also as religio est facer e aliquid do not refer to religious scruples 1 only, but to any qualms of conscience.

After a time, however, religio became more and more defined as the feeling of awe inspired by thoughts of divine powers. Thus Cicero2 states, religio est quae mperioris cujusdam naturae quam divinam vocant curam caerimoniamque affevt, 'Religion is what brings with it the care and cult of some higher power which they call divine.' As we find here religio and caerimonia placed side by side, we find likewise cultus and religio 3 joined, the former expressing the outward, the latter the inward worship of the gods.
A distinction is soon made also between religion and superstition, as Cicero says, nee vero superstitions tollerida4 religio tollitur, 'though superstition should be removed, religion is not.'

Lastly, religio, and also the plural religiones5, became the recognised names of outward religious acts, of cult and ceremony. Thus Cicero ° distinctly explains religio by cidtus deorum, and he declares 7 that the religion of the Romans is divided into sacra, 1 Liv. ii. 62, 'Ut numine aliquo defensa castra oppugnare iterum religio fuerit.'

DEFINITION OF RELIGION. 39 sacrifices, and auBpida, observations of the flight of birds, to which a third part has been added, namely, when the interpreters of the Sibyl or the haruspices declared something for the sake of prophecy from portenta and monstra. The ausjneia he supposes to have been founded by Romulus, the sacra by Numa. In another place he distinguishes superstition from religion, quae deorum cultu pio continetur1, 'which consists in the devout worship of the gods.' We meet even with such expressions as veligio deorum immortalium 2, i. e. the worship of the immortal gods.

So far we can watch the natural development of the word veligio in Latin. It began with the meaning of care, attention, reverence, awe ; it then took the moral sense of scruple and conscience ; and lastly became more and more exclusively applied to the inward feeling of reverence for the gods and to the outward manifestation of that reverence in worship and sacrifice. There are some late writers who use religio in the sense of faith ; for instance, Cassiodorus (died 562, A.D.), Religionem cogere non possumus, quia nemo eogitur ut invitus credat 33 ' We cannot force religion, for no one is ever forced to believe against his will ' : but in classical Latin religio never has that meaning.

Thus ends the biography of the word religio, so long as it lived its natural life, unchequered by technical definition. We can clearly see that what the Romans expressed by religio was chiefly the moral or practical, not the speculative or philosophical side of religion. The questions as to the 1 N. D. i. 42, 117. - Cic. Lad. 25. 96.
3 Variorum Libri, ii. 27.

existence, the character and powers of their gods, did not trouble their minds, so long as they were left to themselves ; still less did they make their sense of moral obligation, which they called religio, dependent on their faith in the gods only. They had a feeling of awe in their hearts at the sight of anything that seemed to them overpowering and beyond the grasp of their senses and their understanding. They did not care much whence that feeling arose, but they called it religio, that is, considering, thinking twice, hesitating ; that was enough for them. The idea that the gods had implanted that feeling in their hearts, or that a thing was wrong or right because the gods had forbidden or commanded it, did not occur to them, till they had come in contact with Greek philosophy. Their religion, if we may use that word in its later and far more general sense, was very much what Spinoza in his Tractatus theologico-politicus thinks that practical religion ought always to be, simple piety and obedience, as distinguished from philosophy and love of knowledge. The gods were accepted without any misgivings, their approval of what was right and good was taken for granted, and no further questions were asked. So great is the difference between religio, as understood by the Romans, and religio as commonly understood, by us, that religio Romana would never have conveyed to Cato the idea of his knowledge of Jupiter, Mars, or Vesta, and the duties he owed to them, but rather that of ancient Roman piety. There is a well-known verse by Schiller:
' Which religion I have ? There is none of all you may mention. Which I embrace, and the cause? Truly, religion it is,'

DEFINITION OF RELIGION. 41 Here he uses religion in the first line in a purely modern sense, in the second line in a truly classical sense. .What he meant was that he was held back by awe, by reverence and humility, from deciding on the truth of any single form of faith, and this the Romans too might have called religion.
French has in some expressions retained the classical meaning of religio. In such a phrase as II a line religion inviolable pour sa parole we recognise the Latin religio jurisjurandi1.
Later meanings of Religio.

We now have to follow the word religio in its later wanderings. Transferred to a Christian soil, religion became really a foreign word, and as such had to be defined by those who used it. and chiefly by theologians and philosophers. We naturally look first to the Old and New Testament to see in what sense religion is used there. But in the translation of the Old Testament the word religion never occurs, and in the New Testament it occurs three times only ; and in one of these passages the translation varies between religion and superstition. In the Acts of the Apostles, xxvi. 5, we read : ' I lived a Pharisee after the most straitest sect of our religion.' Here religion, in the Vulgate, religio, corresponds to the Greek Op-qaKeta, which means outward worship of the gods. In the Epistle of St. James (i. 26, 27), we have dprjo-Kcia, religious worship, and the adjective 6pr\o-Kos, which is rendered by religious, in the Vulgate by religiosus.

Dogmatic Definitions.

We have now to consider the third class of definitions, which I called dogmatic. They differ from the etymological and historical definitions in that they give us the opinions of individuals; whether theologians or philosophers, who take upon themselves to say, not so much what religion does mean or did mean, but what it shall mean. There is generally something dictatorial in such definitions. I open the pages of a philosophical journal2, and I find in close proximity the following definitions of religion : ' Religion is our recognition of the unity of nature, and teaches us to consider ourselves as parts of the whole ; and who can doubt its strong influence upon all our conduct!' On the next page I read, 'Theology and Metaphysics have nothing to do with Morality,' and soon after, ' Religion has never been other than science, plus worship or emotion.'

We can hardly open a book without meeting with similar random definitions of religion. Religion is said to be knowledge, and it is said to be ignorance. Religion is said to be freedom, and it is said to be dependence. Religion is said to be desire, and it is said to be freedom from all desires. Religion is said to be silent contemplation, and it is said to be splendid 1 Ets lavTuv, lib. vi. 30, ed. Gataker, p. 52.
2 The Open Court, vol. i. pp. 978-981.

and stately worship of God. People take every kind of liberty with this old word. Young poets will tell you that poetry is their religion, young artists, that their religion is art, while it has been said of old that ' pure religion is to visit the fatherless and widows in their affliction, and to keep yourselves unspotted from the world V We cannot contest the right of every one to define religion as he understands it. For see how the matter stands with regard to definition. We have the etymological meaning of religion, but that is not binding ; and we have the various historical meanings of religion, and they again are not binding. What criteria then can we discover for testing the truth of what I call the dogmatic definitions of religion ? Some are clearly far too narrow, others far too wide. Some are faulty in themselves, others prove deficient when we try to apply them to historical facts. We must examine the most important of them, and though such an examination, even of the most important definitions only, will no doubt occupy some time, we ought to remember how often a whole dialogue has been devoted by Plato to this kind of philosophical reconnoitring, and ought not to grudge the time which we have to devote to this preliminary inquiry.
Religion and Theology.

In conducting this inquiry we must be careful in the choice and use of our own words, and we must try, as far as possible, to use every word in one sense only. We must distinguish, for instance, between religion and theology, though these words have often 1 Ep. St. James, i. 27.

DEFINITION OF KELIGION. 45 been used promiscuously. By religion we should always understand the subject itself, by theology the study or science of that subject. This terminology, so far as the word theology is concerned, has prevailed ever since the time of Abelard, and there seems to be no reason for changing it.

The Greek word th-eologos was used originally in a different sense. Thus Homer and Hesiod were called theologi (Herodotus, ii. 53), not in the modern sense of theologians, but as conversant with the origin and history of the gods. Hesiod' s Theogony might have been called his Theology, or, at all events, a part of it, and that name is applied to similar works, such as the Theology of Thamyris, and of Orpheus, who is specially called 6 Oeokoyos by the Neo-platonists 1. Plato and Aristotle used theology in the sense of ' doctrine concerning Deity and Divine things,' koyoi irepl rod deov /cat 7rept t&v 6tiQ>v.

In Latin theologia was taken by Varro in the sense of what we call religion, there being according to him three kinds of theology, the mythical, the pltysical, and the civil. The mythical theology contained the fables about the gods, and many things, we are told, contrary to the dignity of immortal beings. The physical theology was described by him as beyond the capacity of the vulgar, while he considered the civil theology, the received religion of Rome, as best for a good citizen to believe.

In Christian phraseology theologos meets us first as the name of the author of the Apocalypse, John the Divine, or the theologos. This name, however, wre are told, was given to him, not simply because he was 1 See Grappe, Die griechischen Quite, ]>[>. t>32-637.
what we call a theologian, but because he maintained the divinity of the Logos. In the third and fourth centuries theologos is said to have meant usually one who defended that doctrine.

Later, and particularly during the middle ages, theology came to mean religious doctrine in general, as studied by theologians or priests, and Abelard's Theologia Christiana was meant to represent what was afterwards called Summa theologlae, a body of systematical knowledge concerning Christian religion 1.
Dogmatic and Practical Religion.

The fashion which prevailed for some time, particularly in Germany, of using religion in the sense of practical and moral religion, while reserving theology as a name of dogmatic religion, is objectionable, and can only create confusion. We may distinguish between dogmatic and practical religion, and we may equally distinguish between dogmatic and practiced theology. But as a theologian is now always used in the sense of a man who studies religion professionally or who belongs to the faculty of theology, it will be best to reserve theology as a name of this study. A mere believer in the dogmas of any religion is not yet a theologian. I therefore propose to retain religion in its general sense, comprising both dogmatic and practical religion, and reserve theology as the name for a scientific study of both. This will prevent all misunderstanding, unless we prefer to drop the name of theology altogether, and replace it by the name of the Science of Religion.
1 8eo Flint, in Encyckp, Brit. s.v. Theology.
DEFINITION OF RELIGION. 47 Comparative Theology.

It is likewise a mere abuse of technical terms to speak of Comparative Religion. There is religion and there is a science of religion, just as there is language and a science of language. But no one would speak of Comparative Language ; neither ought we to speak of Comparative Religion. It is different with mythology. Mythology may be used, not only for a collection of myths, but likewise for a scientific treatment of them, and in the latter sense therefore it would be correct to speak of Comparative Mythology.
Wo have thus far distinguished between : Religion, dogmatic and practical, and Theology, dogmatic and practical.

To some philosophers, and theologians also, such a division between practical and dogmatic religion seems objectionable, nay, impossible, because they maintain that morality cannot possibly exist without some belief in a divine, or, at least, a rational government of the world, and that dogma again would be useless, unless it became the motive of practical morality. This may be true, but we need not enter into that question at present, for by simply qualifying religion as either dogmatic or practical, we only distinguish, we do not separate ; and without committing ourselves as yet to any opinion as to whether morality can exist without dogma or dogma without morality, we do no more by our nomenclature than admit the existence of a common element in both.
Schleiermacher's Definition of Religion.
Some philosophers, however, and particularly Schleiermacher, claim the right of using religion in a still 48 LECTURE II.

higher sense. " They deny that religion is either dogmatic or moral : they deny also that a combination of dogma and morality would give us religion. They point out that when we say that a man is without religion, we do not mean simply that he does not believe in Judaism, Christianity, or any other form of faith, or declines to submit to their moral codes. We mean really that he is without any religious sentiment. Schleiermacher explains religious sentiment as being the immediate consciousness that all that seems finite is infinite, that all that seems temporal is eternal. ' To seek and find what is infinite and eternal in all that lives and moves, in all changes and chances, in all doing and suffering, in fact by an immediate sentiment to have and know life itself as the infinite and eternal life, that,' he says, ' is religion.' — ' From that point of view, if once reached, all events become real miracles, all miracles become real events ; all experience becomes revelation, all revelation experience.'— ' If we do not see our own miracles around us, if we do not perceive within us our own revelations, if our soul does not yearn to draw in the beauty of the whole world and to be pervaded by its spirit ; if in the highest moments of our life we do not feel ourselves impelled by the divine spirit and speaking and acting from our own holy inspiration, if we do not at least feel all that we feel as an immediate influence of the universe, and yet discover in it something that is our own, that cannot be imitated, but can prove its pure origin within ourselves, we have no religion.'

We shall have to consider this meaning of religion when we come to examine the Upanishads, the Ve- DEFINITION OF RELIGION. 49 danta philosophy, the poetry of the Sufis, and the speculations of the mediaeval mystics ; but it seems to me that it would be better if a different name could be assigned to what may be the highest height which religion can reach, but is nevertheless a complete transfiguration rather of human nature than a system of doctrines about the Divine, and a code of precepts inspired by our belief in the Divine. In German it is called Religiositat ; in English religiousness or devotion might be used in the same sense.
Religion, either belief or body of doctrines.

We have still one remark to make with regard to the ordinary use of the word ' religion,' before we can feel ourselves properly equipped for grappling with the great historical definitions of religion which have to be examined. Like many terms of the same character, religion can be used either for our own intellectual possession of theoretic dogmas and moral principles, or as a name of a body of doctrines and precepts collected by authority, chiefly for the purpose of teaching these doctrines and practices. Thus we may say that a person has changed the Jewish for the Christian religion, that is to say, that he has changed his own religious convictions. But we may also say that a person is studying the Buddhist religion, either by reading the sacred books of the Buddhists or by watching the life of the Buddhists in Ceylon or China, without allowing these studies to exercise the least effect on his own convictions. This ambiguity can hardly be avoided, and we have to make allowance for it in all branches of knowledge. We speak of logic, meaning either the laws of thought as E 50 LECTURE II.
we know and follow them ourselves, or a body of doctrines, contained in essays and manuals ; and we shall have to bear in mind the same double meaning when we speak of religion.

A strict adherence to the terminology, as we have now explained it, will help us, I hope, to avoid many misunderstandings, and enable us at the same time to assign to each of the various definitions of religion its proper place.
zh: 宗教的定義，為何需要此定義。
如果宗教學要被視為一門自然科學，那麼我們顯然必須從仔細蒐集事實開始，這些事實應能闡明宗教的起源、成長與衰亡。
但我們會發現，除非我們先進行一項初步的——我必須補充，略為困難的——探究，即「宗教」究竟意指為何，否則便無法做到這點。除非我們能對這一點達成清晰的共識，否則在為宗教研究蒐集證據時，我們將無法確定該納入哪些事實、排除哪些事實。

那麼，什麼是宗教？對許多人而言，這聽起來像是一個非常簡單的問題，就像「什麼是人？」這個問題一樣簡單。務實的人反對這類問題，並認為任何試圖回答它們的努力都純屬浪費時間。誠然，存在一種公眾輿論，它為所有日常用途界定了詞語的意義，我們在日常生活的關切中或許可以讓自己受其引導。但在哲學討論中，這是嚴格禁止的。哲學若不是對語言進行持續的批判與修正，而哲學史若不是對古老且熟悉的術語接連賦予新定義的過程，那又是什麼呢？
*定義宗教時的重大分歧。
此外，關於宗教的真正意義，可說毫無共識可言。大多數人無論在其他觀點上立場為何，大概都會認為宗教必然與神或諸神有所關聯。但即便如此也非定論。例如佛教——這個由最多人類所信奉的信仰體系——根據釋迦牟尼的教導，既不承認有神，至少不承認有宇宙的創造者；因此有人主張，佛教不能被稱為宗教。

在我們能夠回答「佛教是否為一種宗教？」這個問題之前，我們必須先對「宗教」一詞的定義達成共識。如果我們將宗教理解為對某種至高存在——無論是稱為上帝、耶和華、梵天（Brahmā），還是阿拉——的信仰，那麼佛教顯然不是一種宗教。佛陀從未承認任何至高存在；他否認吠陀（Veda）的權威，否認婆羅門的祭司職權，否認任何形式的祈禱或獻祭。他教導的是一條道德自律與內在修行的道路，一條最終導向涅槃（Nirvāṇa）——即一切慾望與痛苦的熄滅——的道路。 然而，如果我們以更寬廣的視角來看待宗教，將其理解為人類心靈對無限者（the Infinite）的感知與回應，那麼佛教無疑是一種宗教。事實上，佛教或許比其他任何宗教體系都更徹底地展現了人類對超越性真理的追尋。它從印度教的土壤中萌芽，卻以一種革命性的姿態，將宗教從儀式與神話的束縛中解放出來，將其轉化為一種純粹的倫理體系與心靈哲學。

現在，我們確實可以如此定義宗教，以致這個名稱無法應用於佛教；但問題是，誰有權利如此狹隘地限縮「宗教」一詞的定義，使其不再適用於大多數人類的信仰？各位明白，定義權是一項極為神聖的權利，若我們希望避免純粹文字之爭（logomachies）的危險，就必須審慎守護它。我常被問到：你稱佛陀的宗教為宗教嗎？你稱達爾文的哲學為哲學嗎？或稱華格納的音樂為音樂嗎？在這種挑釁下，除了回答「請定義你所說的宗教、定義你所說的哲學、定義你所說的音樂，然後，唯有在那之後，我們才可能達成共識，判斷佛陀的教義能否稱為宗教、達爾文的著作能否稱為哲學、華格納的作品能否稱為音樂」之外，我們還能說什麼？我深知，沒有什麼比要求對方提出定義更能激怒對手；然而，眾所周知（或理應眾所周知），定義正是古代哲學家——蘇格拉底、柏拉圖與亞里斯多德——哲學的基石，而缺乏恰當的定義，過去是、現在依然是現代哲學的禍根¹。

「定義」一詞本身便需要定義。若我們翻開任何一部邏輯學手冊，便會發現「定義」（definitio）被描述為一種陳述，用以揭示一物之本質（essentia）。然而，此處所謂的「本質」究竟何指？它是指事物之真實本性（natura），抑或僅是我們對該事物所形成的概念（notio）？當我們為宗教學（the science of religion）下定義時，我們所欲界定的，並非某種可觸可摸之物，而是一系列現象——這些現象雖可觀察，卻源於人類心靈最深處的活動。因此，我們必須謹慎區分：我們所定義的，究竟是宗教本身（religio），還是我們對宗教的科學研究（scientia religionis）？
但在我們能為宗教下定義之前，必須先為「定義」本身下定義——儘管這項要求看似迂腐。
至少有三種定義：語源學定義、歷史定義與教義定義。

「宗教學」（the science of religion）這個名稱，若從其詞源來理解，或許會引導我們去尋找某種將所有宗教連結起來的紐帶，某種將它們統攝於一個概念之下的共同元素。然而，這樣的期望恐怕會落空。拉丁文 *religio* 這個詞的詞源至今仍無定論。西塞羅（Cicero）將它與 *relegere*（重新閱讀、反覆思索）聯繫起來，而拉克坦提烏斯（Lactantius）則認為它源自 *religare*（重新綁縛、連結）。這兩種解釋都各有其支持者，但沒有一種能令人完全信服。事實上，*religio* 這個詞在拉丁文中的用法極為廣泛，從對神靈的敬畏、對儀式的恪守，到迷信、顧忌，甚至於對某項事務的嚴格遵守，都可稱之為 *religio*。因此，試圖從詞源中提煉出一個放諸四海皆準的宗教定義，恐怕是徒勞無功的。我們最好還是直接觀察那些被稱為「宗教」的現象本身，並嘗試從中歸納出它們的共同特徵。

許多人仍以為詞源學本身就等同於定義。這種觀念在早期曾廣泛盛行，那時詞源學的真正原則尚未被發現；而時至今日，儘管已毫無藉口，這種觀念依然存在。例如，荷馬非常喜歡用詞源學來解釋某些神祇與英雄的特殊性格。柏拉圖更進一步擴展了這種做法，儘管他經常讓我們懷疑，他對自己的詞源學解釋究竟是否認真。各位知道，他在《克拉底魯斯篇》（410）中如何將 ἀήρ（空氣）從 αἴρειν（舉起）推導而來，視之為將事物從地面舉起的元素；他又如何解釋 αἰθήρ（以太）為 ἀεὶ θεῖν（永遠流動），因為這個元素總是環繞空氣流動（ἀεὶ δεῖ ἀέρα ῥέον）。他將 θεοί（諸神）也從同一詞根 θεῖν（奔跑）推導出來，因為他懷疑——如他所說——「太陽、月亮、大地、星辰與天空，這些至今仍……」¹ ¹ 參見彌爾《宗教三論》，第4頁。
2 參見《數論真諦月光疏》（Sārikhyatattvakaumudī）第四節；其中「tannirvacanam ca lakṣaṇam」意為「詞源解釋即定義」。
3 Cratylus, 397 C.
許多野蠻人的神祇，是原初希臘人唯一知曉的神明；由於他們觀察到這些神祇總是在移動與奔跑，便根據這種奔跑的本性，稱之為神或奔跑者；後來，當他們發現所有其他神祇時，仍保留了這個舊稱。」亞里斯多德在其詞源定義上較為節制，但他同樣將「以太」（αἰθήρ）一詞歸源於「永遠奔跑」（ἀεὶ θεῖν），因為它總是在流動與運行¹。
科曼人效法了希臘人的做法。2. 像盧克萊修與奧維德這樣的詩人，每當詞源學似乎與他們的觀點相符時，便沈溺於其中；直至晚期，羅馬法學家仍樂於藉由或多或少帶有想像力的衍生詞，來支持他們對法律術語的定義。
在印度，這些詞源定義也從最早時期便獲得承認。它們通常以下列方式引入：「這是鞍之為鞍，因我們坐於其上」；「這是路之為路，因我們行於其上」；「這是天（heaven）之為天，因它被高舉（heaved）於上。」只不過，儘管這些詞源在歷史上是正確的，但對於《梵書》（Brāhmaṇa）或《尼祿多》（Nirukta）的作者而言，任何詞源解釋只要能夠說明該詞的某種意義，便皆受歡迎。
在某些情況下，這些詞源學定義非常有用，但需要極其審慎。首先，許多民間詞源學在語音上站不住腳，在歷史上也是錯誤的。例如，God（神）這個詞——參見《論宇宙》（Περὶ Κόσμου），迪多版，卷三，第 628 頁，第 28 行；「關於神」（Στάτο δέ θεῖον）。
2 Lersch，《古人語言哲學》，第三卷；西塞羅《論神性》卷三，第24節。
3 參見《學院》（Academy），1888 年 12 月；亦見普魯塔克（Plutarch），《殘篇》（Fragm.）21. 27。
4 Varro. LI. v. 7. ed. Egger. • 四個解釋層級：最低者即連平民也能理解。誰看不出採沙場與道路養護的由來？』Lersch, 前揭書，卷三，頁126。
「善」這個詞，不可能從「神」衍生而來，因為語音規律不允許如此，而且這兩個詞是平行發展的，就我們所能追溯的歷史來看，它們從未相互接近。

但即使某個詞源在語音與歷史層面上無可挑剔，它所能提供的，也絕不會超過該詞最初的起點。它或許能告訴我們，被命名的事物最初是如何被構想的，但僅此而已。我們知道，例如拉丁文的 *deus* 對應梵文的 *deva*（或許也對應希臘文的 *θεός*，儘管這兩種詞源學解釋皆未嚴格符合語音規則¹），而 *deva* 原意為「明亮的」。這點極為重要，因為它向我們揭示：關於神聖者的諸多概念中，有一支是從「明亮且仁慈的存在者」這個概念出發的——諸如太陽、月亮與星辰，與黑夜陰暗致命的面貌相對立；但若以為這能幫助我們理解像帕斯卡這樣的思想家心中對「上帝」的概念，那就荒謬了。我們若能發現一個詞的萌芽概念——例如證明 *deus* 原本不過是「明亮的存在者」、祭司原本是「年長者」、牧師是「僕人」、主教是「監督者」——對此我們感激不盡；然而，若我們將這些詞源學解釋視為超越歷史趣聞的東西，並誤以為它們就是定義，那隻會證明我們對語言本質的無知。語言始終處於漲落不息的狀態，並且比自然界的任何其他部分都更能向我們展現持續演化的過程。
1 參見《選集》卷一，第215頁。我至今仍堅持該處所表達的觀點。

宗教學的真正起點，在於我們能夠以歷史的眼光來看待宗教——也就是說，將宗教視為人類心智在特定時間與地點條件下的產物，並追溯其從最粗糙的開端到當今最精緻形式的發展過程。穆勒（Müller）主張，唯有透過比較方法，我們才能理解宗教的本質；正如語言科學（the science of language）藉由比較各種語言而揭示其共同法則，宗教學（the science of religion）也必須比較不同民族的信仰與實踐，方能發現隱藏於其多樣性背後的統一原理。他強調，宗教並非來自天啟的靜態真理，而是人類回應「無限者」（the Infinite）之經驗的不斷演進的表現——這種經驗最初以模糊的直覺出現，隨後在吠陀（Veda）的讚歌中、在奧義書（Upanishad）的沉思裡、以及在閃語族（Semitic）與雅利安（Aryan）民族的祈禱中，逐漸獲得清晰的形態。因此，歷史定義下的宗教學，其任務不在於評判某種宗教的真偽，而在於客觀地描述宗教現象的起源、變遷與相互影響，並從中歸納出宗教意識發展的普遍規律。

現在我們來談歷史定義。我所謂的歷史定義，乃是對詞語意義所經歷的種種變遷所作的記述——只要這些變遷仍任由同一語言廣大使用群體中那些無聲且無意識的影響力所主導。因此，若要為 *deus* 下一個歷史定義，就必須展示從 *cleva*（明亮）——用以形容太陽、黎明及其他天象——到提婆（Devas），即這些天體之內或背後的力量，最後再到印度人稱之為提婆（Devas）、羅馬人稱之為 *dii* 的、自然之中或超越自然的仁慈施為者——這一系列變遷過程。正如一個人的傳記可被稱為其最佳定義，我所謂的「詞語傳記」，或許正是我們所能提供的最有用的定義。

> 「宗教學」這個名稱，雖然現在已普遍使用，但仍有許多誤解。有些人認為它意味著一種宗教體系，類似於神學體系；有些人則將其與比較神學（comparative theology）混為一談；還有人甚至將其視為一種試圖將所有宗教融合為一體的努力。然而，宗教學（the science of religion）的真正意義，與語言科學（the science of language）或任何其他歸納科學並無二致。它僅僅是對宗教現象進行系統性的研究，正如語言科學是對語言現象進行系統性的研究一樣。它的目標是發現宗教的本質、形式、發展與規律，而非建立任何特定的教義體系。
最後是教義式定義，我以此指稱那些基於個人權威所下的定義——無論某詞在詞源上原意為何，也無論其在歷史演變中獲得何種意義，這些定義者宣稱：為其自身目的，他們打算以某種特定含義使用該詞。此舉主要見於哲學家、法學家與科學家，他們無法容忍重要詞彙承載著詞源與歷史意義的所有模糊性，因而通常藉由古老的邏輯方法——釐清其屬類與種差——一勞永逸地決定這些詞彙未來應被使用的精確意涵。
現在讓我們來看看這三種定義方式如何被應用在我們必須處理的這個詞上，也就是宗教。 DEFINITION OF RELIGION. 33

「宗教學」（the science of religion）這個名稱，若從其最廣義的層面來理解，或許會引發一些反對意見。然而，我認為我們可以毫無困難地接受它，只要我們清楚界定其範疇，並將其與「比較神學」（comparative theology）區分開來。後者在我看來，只是宗教學的一個分支，儘管是極為重要的分支。宗教學，就其最完整的意義而言，不僅包含比較神學，也包含比較神話學（comparative mythology），以及對宗教的哲學與歷史研究。它試圖探究宗教的本質、起源與發展，並追蹤其從最原始的形式到最高形式的演變過程。 在我們深入探討宗教學的具體問題之前，或許值得先釐清「宗教」（religio）這個詞本身的語源學含義。這個詞的拉丁文起源，長久以來一直是學者們爭論的焦點。西塞羅（Cicero）將它與 *relegere*（重新閱讀、重新審視）聯繫起來，意指對神聖事物的謹慎反覆思考。另一方面，拉克坦提烏斯（Lactantius）和聖奧古斯丁（St. Augustine）則傾向於將其與 *religare*（重新綁縛、連結）聯繫起來，認為宗教是將人與神重新連結在一起的紐帶。現代語源學家則提出了另一種可能性，將其與 *relinguere*（留下、遺留）相關聯，暗示宗教是對某些被遺留下來、被視為神聖之物的關注。 無論其確切的語源為何，重要的是要認識到，這個詞在歷史的長河中承載了極為豐富且多變的內涵。它不僅僅指涉一套外在的儀式或教條，更觸及人類心靈深處對「無限者」（the Infinite）的感知與回應。這種感知，正如我們將在後續講座中看到的，是宗教最根本的基礎。

宗教的詞源學定義之所以引起神學家們相當大的興趣，乃是出於一種隱含的信念：這個詞的詞源，無論如何，都必定有助於揭示其真實含義。眾所周知，拉克坦提烏斯（Lactantius）將「religio」溯源於「religare」，意為「束縛」或「約束」；他這麼做，並非單純出於語文學家的身分，而是作為一位神學家。他說：「我們生來就處於這樣的條件之下：當我們出生時，就應當向神獻上我們理所當然應盡的侍奉，應當唯獨認識祂，唯獨跟隨祂。我們因敬虔的紐帶而被繫於神、與祂相連（religati），宗教（religion）這個名稱正是由此而來，而非如西塞羅（Cicero）所解釋的那樣，來自於『專注』（a relegehdo）。」

在檢視此一詞源之前，先列出拉克坦提烏斯（Lactantius）歸於西塞羅（Cicero）的詞源解釋，並為他所大膽駁斥，將頗有助益。西塞羅說道：「凡仔細處理一切與諸神崇拜相關事務者，稱為『虔敬者』（religiosi），源自『反覆閱讀』（relegere）一詞——正如『優雅者』（elegantes）得名於『挑選』（elegere），『勤勉者』（diligentes）得名於『選擇、珍視』（diligere），而『智者』（intelligentes）則得名於『洞察』（intellegere）。」¹ ¹ 拉克坦提烏斯，《神聖原理》（Institutiones Divinae）卷四，第28章：「我們生而具有此一條件：受造之身應向神獻上公正與應有的順服，唯獨認識祂、跟隨祂。我們因這敬虔的紐帶而受縛於神、與神相繫（religati）；宗教（religio）之名即由此而來，而非如西塞羅所解釋的，來自『反覆閱讀』（relegendo）。」
2 而是源自一個已失傳的動詞 *elegare*。

> 「宗教，就其最廣泛的意義而言，乃是人類心靈對某種無限者的感知與回應——無論這無限者被稱為神、法則、精神，或是宇宙本身。」——穆勒 我們首先必須釐清「宗教學」（the science of religion）這門學科的確切範圍。它並非神學（theology），後者預設了特定啟示與教義體系；宗教學乃是一門比較性的歷史科學，旨在透過對人類所有宗教現象的客觀研究，揭示宗教的本質與發展規律。正如語言科學（the science of language）透過比較不同語系來追溯語言的起源與演化，宗教學也必須透過比較神學（comparative theology）與比較神話學（comparative mythology）的方法，來理解宗教觀唸的生成與轉變。 關於宗教的起源，存在兩種極端觀點：一種認為宗教是神聖啟示的直接產物，另一種則將其貶為原始人類的幻覺或欺騙。然而，真正的科學態度要求我們追問：人類究竟如何獲得「神」或「無限者」（the Infinite）的概念？這個問題引導我們回到語言的根源。在吠陀（Veda）文獻中，我們看到雅利安人（Aryan）如何從自然現象中提煉出神聖概念——例如，天空之神特尤斯（Dyaus）這個詞，在梵文中意為「照耀者」，它與希臘文的Ζεύς、拉丁文的Jupiter同源，最終演變為「神」的普遍稱謂。這絕非偶然，而是語言與思想相互作用的典型例證。 我曾在別處提出，神話本質上是「語言的疾病」（a disease of language）。這並非貶抑神話，而是指出一個關鍵事實：當原始人類用來描述自然現象的隱喻性詞彙，其原始意義被遺忘時，這些詞彙便獲得了獨立的人格化生命。例如，梵文中的「提婆」（Deva，意為「光明者」）原本只是形容太陽或天空的屬性，但隨著時間推移，它變成了獨立的神祇。這種語言上的誤解與再詮釋，正是多神教（polytheism）與輪換主神教（kathenotheism）——即在不同情境下輪流崇拜不同至高神祇的現象——的重要來源。 透過比較研究，我們可以辨識出宗教意識的幾個典型階段： 1. **多神教（polytheism）**：將自然力量視為眾多獨立神祇。 2. **單一神教（henotheism）**：在崇拜某一特定神祇時，將其視為至高無上，但並不否認其他神祇的存在。梨俱吠陀（Rig-Veda）中許多讚歌即屬此類。 3. **一神教（monotheism）**：明確承認唯一至高神，否定其他神祇的真實性。 值得注意的是，這些階段並非線性進化，而是經常交錯並存。閃語族（Semitic）宗教傳統（如猶太教、基督教、伊斯蘭教）傾向於一神教，而雅利安（Aryan）與圖蘭語族（Turanian）的宗教則展現出更複雜的多神與單一神教混合形態。 在印度思想的最高成就——奧義書（Upanishad）中，我們看到宗教思想從儀式主義向哲學思辨的深刻轉變。奧義書的聖者們不再滿足於崇拜眾神，而是追問：那使太陽發光、使風吹動、使思想運作的終極實在是什麼？他們的答案是「梵」（Brahman）——那超越一切名稱與形式的無限者。這種對終極統一的追尋，標誌著宗教從神話向哲學的過渡，也為比較宗教學提供了極其珍貴的素材。 宗教學必須遵循嚴格的歷史比較方法。我們不能預設某種宗教為真理標準，而將其他宗教視為謬誤；相反，我們應當像植物學家研究不同植物那樣，客觀描述每一宗教的結構、發展與功能。這需要我們掌握原始文獻的語言——梵文、巴利文、阿維斯陀語、希伯來語、希臘語、拉丁語等——並透過語源學分析，追溯宗教觀唸的演變軌跡。唯有如此，宗教學才能真正成為一門科學，而非神學的附庸或啟蒙運動的偏見產物。
讓我們先釐清一些一再重複、但實際上毫無根據的說法。常有人說瓦羅（Varro）支持拉克坦提烏斯（Lactantius）的詞源學，但瓦羅僅僅討論了 *legere* 與 *legio*，因此間接支持的是西塞羅（Cicero）的詞源學，而非拉克坦提烏斯的。
> 再者，若真要引用費斯圖斯（Festus）對 *relligio* 一詞所提出的詞源解釋，他其實是站在西塞羅（Cicero）那一邊，而非拉克坦提烏斯（Lactantius）。因為費斯圖斯指出，人們之所以被稱為 *religiosi*（虔敬者），乃是因為他們根據國家習俗，在敬拜諸神時對應行或應避免之事做出選擇（*delectus*），並且不讓自己陷入迷信之中³。
後世作家中，聖奧古斯丁（St. Augustin）有時依循此一詞源，有時又採納另一詞源，端視其論述目的而定；而在近代神學家之間，竟有人主張「宗教」（religio）同時源自 *vellgare* 與 *relegere*，以便將兩者之義結合起來。

從純粹語文學的觀點來看，西塞羅《論神性》ii. 28 中寫道：「至於那些仔細審視並彷彿重新閱讀一切與敬神相關之事的人，他們被稱為『虔誠者』（religiosi），這個詞來自『重新閱讀』（relegendo），正如『優雅者』（elegantes）來自『選擇』（eligendo），『勤勉者』（diligentes）來自『熱愛』（diligendo），以及『明智者』（intelligentes）來自『理解』（intelligendo）。因為在這些詞語中，都蘊含著與『虔誠地』（religiose）相同的『閱讀』（legendi）之意。」
2 Varro, *De lingua latina* v. 68；ed. Egger。Legio，「因士兵在選拔中被選出」；Nitzsch，《研究與評論》，第一卷，第527頁。
Festus，第 236 頁：「所謂『有宗教者』（Religiosi），是指那些依照城邦習俗，對於神聖事務之應行與應止有所抉擇，且不使自己糾纏於迷信（superstitionibus）之中的人。」
4 ‘Relegendo se sentit religatum’，von Drey，引自 Nitzsch，同上。

不能否認 *religio* 可能源自 *religare*，就如同源自 *religere* 一樣。一般反對意見認為，從 *religare* 應得出 *religatio*，而非 *religio*，這其實沒有真正的說服力，因為我們在 *opinari* 旁邊發現了 *opinio*（而非 *opinatio*）與 *necopinus* 這類詞；在 *rebellare* 旁邊則有 *rebellis* 與 *rebellio*。同樣地，在 *lictor* 一詞中，若其原意是指「捆綁罪犯的人」，我們也必須承認在 *ligare* 旁邊存在一個詞根 *ligere*。

將 *religio* 歸源於 *religare* 的真正反對理由，在於古典拉丁文中 *religare* 從未用於「束縛」或「約束」之意。若指此義，我們理應見到 *obligatio*，或可能為 *obligio*，而非 *religio*。因此，西塞羅的詞源學說顯然更為可取，因其更符合拉丁慣用語法。*Relegere* 應為 *neglegere* 或 *negligere*¹ 的反義詞；既然 *neglegere* 意為「不關心」，*relegere* 自然便意指「關心」、「重視」、「敬畏」²。根據尼基狄烏斯‧菲古盧斯（Nigidius Figulus）所引述的一位古代作家詩句——該詩句由格利烏斯（Gellius, iv. 9）保存——我們得知 *religeus* 實際上曾被用作 *religiosus* 的對立詞。該句雲：「*Religentem esse oportet, religiosus ne fuas*」——「應當敬畏，但勿虔誠」，亦即勿迷信³。
1 字母 e 轉變為 i 是歷史性的。我們看到 *neglego* 與 *negligo*、*intellego* 與 *intelligo*。拼寫為 e 的是古老形式，但也有一些現代複合詞始終保留 e，例如 *perlego*、*intellego*。
2 M. M. 希伯特講座，第 22 頁。

3 格利烏斯（Gellius），赫茲（Hertz）編，卷四：「以 *-osus* 結尾的形容詞通常暗示過度，例如 *vinosus*（嗜酒的）、*mulierosus*（好色的）。因此尼基狄烏斯‧菲古魯斯（Nigidius Figulus）曾說：『這類詞語的結尾始終表示某種過度的豐富，如 *vinosus*、*mulierosus*、*religiosus*，意指其所描述的事物具有過度的分量。因此，*religiosus* 這個詞被用來稱呼那些因過度且迷信的宗教而束縛自己的人，而這種情況被歸為一種缺點。』」
德文 *Andacht* 一詞，字面意為「深思」（thoughtfulness），而後引申為「敬畏」（reverence），有時被拿來與 *religio* 相比較。然而兩者之間存在細微差異，因為 *Andacht* 傳達的意涵更側重於「冥想」（meditation），而非「關注與敬畏」（regard and reverence）。
> 格利烏斯（iv. 9）將宗教的另一個詞源定義歸於一位名為馬蘇裡烏斯‧薩比努斯者。他將 *religiosum*（神聖之意）溯源於 *relinquere*（遺留或擱置），指某物因過於神聖而不適用於日常用途¹。由於語音規律不允許此種詞源推導，我們無須進一步討論。
關於 *religio* 的詞源學，至此已說明完畢；就其最初的概念而言，它只能意指尊敬、關懷與敬畏。

「宗教」一詞的歷史定義，必須從其拉丁文源頭 *religio* 著手探究。西塞羅（Cicero）將 *religio* 追溯至 *relegere*（意為「重新審視」或「反覆閱讀」），暗示一種謹慎的敬畏態度；而拉克坦提烏斯（Lactantius）則主張其源自 *religare*（意為「重新束縛」），強調人與神之間的連結。然而，這些詞源學解釋雖具啟發性，卻不足以涵蓋宗教學（the science of religion）所需的精確範疇。在歷史脈絡中，*religio* 最初指涉的是羅馬公民對傳統儀式與神聖義務的嚴格遵守，而非一套抽象信仰體系。隨著基督教興起，這個詞逐漸內化，轉而強調內在虔誠與教義正統。因此，若要為宗教學奠定基礎，我們必須超越單一文化的詞源侷限，轉向比較神學（comparative theology）與比較神話學（comparative mythology）所提供的跨文化視野。唯有透過語言科學（the science of language）的輔助，釐清雅利安（Aryan）、閃語族（Semitic）與圖蘭語族（Turanian）傳統中對「無限者」（the Infinite）的不同表述——例如吠陀（Veda）中的特尤斯（Dyaus）與提婆（Deva），或奧義書（Upanishad）中的哲學思辨——我們才能避免將「語言的疾病」（a disease of language）誤認為宗教的本質。從單一神教（henotheism）與輪換主神教（kathenotheism）的階段，到一神教（monotheism）與多神教（polytheism）的對比，歷史定義必須承認：宗教並非靜態的實體，而是人類回應神聖者時，所展現的多樣且動態的現象。

現在我們要談到我所謂的歷史定義，或者其他人可能更偏好稱之為「宗教」（religio）一詞在其原生土壤內命運的歷史描述。大多數詞語，尤其是那些構成爭論主題的詞語，都有其自身的歷史。它們的意義隨著世紀更迭而改變，往往一代之間就有所不同；不僅如此，就像人類臉上的表情一樣，一個詞語的表情也可能隨時改變。從某種意義上說，我們的歷史定義可以稱為「praeter ista」的傳記——因此格利烏斯繼續說道：「——尼基狄烏斯所推導出的那些，經由某種意義上的岔路，『虔誠者』（religiosus）開始被用來指稱純潔、謹守、並以特定律法與界限約束自身的人。」
> 1 「然而，馬蘇裡烏斯‧薩比努斯在其所撰寫的《論本土神靈》評註中說道：『所謂「宗教性的」（religiosum），乃是因其某種神聖性而遠離我們、與我們隔絕之物；這個詞源自「遺留」（relinquendo），正如「儀式」（caerimonia）源自「匱乏」（carendo）。』」——格利烏斯，赫茲編校本，卷四，第九章。
一個詞語，只要能夠以近乎完整的方式重新找回其歷史，這樣一部傳記所傳達給我們的資訊，遠比任何邏輯或詞源學定義所能收集到的更為豐富。

只要「religio」這個詞還停留在羅馬的土壤上，其意義的所有變化似乎都完全可以理解——只要我們考慮到那些決定所有詞語意義生長的影響力。後來，當「religio」這個詞從羅馬的氛圍轉移到基督教的氛圍，從古典拉丁語轉移到中世紀拉丁語及現代羅曼語族方言，從民間用語轉移到技術性的神學領域，情況就變得不同了。我們於是進入純粹教條式或任意的定義，語言的天然生長似乎受到阻礙，我們所能做的，就是記錄那些具有權威與影響力的哲學家與神學家，賦予「宗教」一詞的各種意義。
追溯 *religio* 的歷史，我們發現它在拉丁文中原始且較廣泛的意義是「顧念」或「尊敬」，例如在 *religio jurisjurandi*（對誓言的敬重）這類表述中，它與 *metus deorum*（對諸神的畏懼）有所區別¹。

Religio 與 metus 經常一同出現，例如西塞羅《控告威勒斯》第二卷 4, 45, 101 寫道：*ut eam (cupiditatem) non metus, non religio contineret*，此處我們可將 metus 與 religio 二詞譯為「恐懼」與「敬畏」——恐懼指對人或後果的害怕，敬畏則指對諸神的畏懼。另一處提到，當月蝕突然發生時¹，西塞羅《為豐特伊烏斯辯護》ix. 30 寫道：『難道我們真以為，這些部族在作證時會因誓言的 religio 與不朽諸神的 metus 而動搖嗎？他們與其他民族的習俗與本性差異如此之大。』 ¹ 西塞羅《為豐特伊烏斯辯護》ix. 30。
清晰之夜，全軍因敬畏與恐懼而動搖（religione et metu）。諸如「religio est facere aliquid」這類表述，不僅指涉宗教上的顧慮，也涵蓋任何良心的不安。

經過一段時間後，*religio* 逐漸被定義為由神聖力量之思所引發的敬畏感。因此西塞羅（Cicero）陳述道：*religio est quae superioris cujusdam naturae quam divinam vocant curam caerimoniamque affert*，意即「宗教是伴隨著對某種他們稱為神聖的更高本質之關懷與禮儀。」正如我們在此處看到 *religio* 與 *caerimonia* 並列，我們同樣發現 *cultus* 與 *religio* 被連結在一起，前者表達對諸神的外在崇拜，後者則表達內在崇拜。
宗教與迷信之間也很快被劃出界線，正如西塞羅所說：*nee vero superstitione tollenda religio tollitur*，「縱使迷信應被去除，宗教亦不隨之消亡。」

最後，**religio**（以及複數形式的 **religiones**⁵）成為外在宗教行為、禮拜與儀式的公認名稱。因此，西塞羅⁶明確地以 *cultus deorum*（對諸神的崇拜）來解釋 **religio**，並宣稱⁷羅馬人的宗教分為 *sacra*（聖儀）、¹ *auspicia*（佔兆）與 *prodigia*（異象）。⁸ 在普林尼（Pliny）的著作中，我們讀到：「*Elephanti … religionem praestant*」⁹——大象展現出一種宗教敬畏。而在李維（Livy）的筆下，¹⁰ 我們看到：「*ut numine aliquo defensa castra oppugnare iterum religio fuerit*」——由於某種神聖力量的庇護，再次進攻營寨成了一種宗教禁忌。
3 Cic. JV. D. i. 43, 121, 'Quis aut cultu aut religione dignaa judicare (imagines).'
4 Be Div. ii. 72, 148.
5 西塞羅，《威勒斯演說》第二卷，第五篇，第13章，第34節：「違背天理、違背佔兆、違背一切神聖與人間的宗教義務。」
「宗教，即對諸神的崇拜，遠為優越。」
De Nat. Deor. iii. 1：「既然全體羅馬人民的宗教被劃分為聖儀與佔兆，而第三項附屬者，乃是西比爾的解釋者或腸卜師基於異象與怪物所預示的任何預言性告誡。」

犧牲（sacrifices）與占卜（auspicia），即觀察鳥類飛行，後來又加入了第三部分，亦即當西比爾（Sibyl）的解釋者或腸卜師（haruspices）基於預兆（portenta）與異象（monstra）為預言而宣告某事時。他認為占卜（auspicia）由羅慕路斯（Romulus）創立，聖禮（sacra）則由努馬（Numa）創立。在另一處，他區分了迷信與宗教，後者「包含在對諸神的虔誠崇拜中」（quae deorum cultu pio continetur）。我們甚至遇到像「不朽諸神的宗教」（veligio deorum immortalium）這樣的表述，亦即對不朽諸神的崇拜。

到目前為止，我們可以觀察拉丁文 *religio* 一詞的自然發展歷程。它最初意指關心、注意、敬畏、畏懼；隨後取得道德層面的躊躇與良知之意；最後越來越專門地用以指涉對諸神的內在敬畏之情，以及此種敬畏在外在崇拜與獻祭中的表現。有些晚期作家將 *religio* 用於信仰之意，例如卡西奧多羅斯（Cassiodorus，卒於公元562年）寫道：*Religionem cogere non possumus, quia nemo cogitur ut invitus credat*³³「我們無法強迫宗教，因為沒有人會被強迫違背自己的意願去相信」；但在古典拉丁文中，*religio* 從未具有此種含義。
「宗教」（religio）一詞的傳記，在其自然生命歷程中，始終未受技術性定義的幹擾，至此便告終結。我們可以清楚看到，羅馬人以「religio」所表達的，主要是宗教的道德或實踐層面，而非思辨或哲學層面。至於那些關於……的問題¹，則屬另一回事。 --- ¹ 西塞羅《論神性》卷一，42、117；西塞羅《論占卜》25、96。
3 Variorum Libri, ii. 27.

存在、他們諸神的性格與權能——只要他們不受幹擾，這些問題並未困擾他們的心靈；他們更不會將自己所稱的「religio」——即道德義務感——僅僅依附於對諸神的信仰。他們心中懷有一種敬畏之情，每當目睹任何看似壓倒性、超越其感官與理解力的事物時，便油然而生。他們不太在意這種情感從何而來，卻稱之為「religio」，亦即「深思、再三考量、猶豫不決」；對他們而言，這便足夠了。諸神將這種情感植入他們心中，或者事物之所以為善為惡乃因諸神禁止或命令——這樣的想法，在他們接觸希臘哲學之前，從未浮現。他們的宗教——若我們可用該詞的後期且遠為普遍的意義——非常接近斯賓諾莎在其《神學政治論》中所認為的實踐宗教應有之貌：單純的虔敬與順從，有別於哲學與對知識的熱愛。諸神被毫無疑慮地接受，他們對正當與良善之事的認可被視為理所當然，無人進一步追問。羅馬人所理解的「religio」與我們普遍理解的「religio」之間，差異如此之大，以至於「羅馬人的宗教」（religio Romana）在加圖心中絕不會引發他對朱比特、馬爾斯或維斯塔的知識以及他對這些神祇所負義務的概念，反而更指向古羅馬的虔敬。席勒有一句著名的詩句：
『我擁有哪一種宗教？你所提及的一切，我皆無所屬。我所信奉者為何，其因由何在？誠然，宗教本身即是。』
此處他在第一行以純粹現代意義使用「宗教」，第二行則以真正的古典意義使用。他的意思是，他因敬畏、崇敬與謙卑而受阻，無法對任何單一信仰形式的真理性做出裁決——而這，羅馬人也許同樣會稱之為「宗教」。
法語在某些表達中保留了 *religio* 的古典意義。在「Il a une religion inviolable pour sa parole」（他對自己的諾言懷有不可侵犯的敬意）這類短語中，我們辨識出拉丁文的 *religio jurisjurandi*¹（誓言之宗教約束力）。

然而，在羅馬作家筆下，*religio* 一詞的意義已開始轉變，並逐漸獲得我們今日所理解的「宗教」之內涵。西塞羅（Cicero）將 *religio* 定義為「對神明應有的崇敬與禮拜」（*deorum cultu pio et iusto*），並將其與迷信（*superstitio*）區分開來。他認為，*religio* 是對神明謹慎而理性的敬拜，而迷信則是過度且非理性的恐懼。這種區分在後來的拉丁基督教作家中得以延續，並進一步深化。拉克坦提烏斯（Lactantius）將 *religio* 詞源追溯至 *religare*（「束縛」），意謂將人與神連結的紐帶；而奧古斯丁（Augustine）則在駁斥異教時，強調真正的 *religio* 在於對獨一真神的敬拜。由此，*religio* 一詞逐漸脫離其原始的「謹慎」或「顧慮」之意，轉而指涉一套關於神聖事物的信仰與實踐體系，亦即我們今日所稱的「宗教」。

我們現在必須追隨「religio」一詞其後的流變歷程。當這個詞被移植到基督教的土壤上時，「宗教」實際上變成了一個外來語，因此使用它的人——主要是神學家和哲學家——必須對其加以定義。我們自然會先查考《舊約》與《新約》，看看「宗教」一詞在其中是如何使用的。然而，在《舊約》的譯本中，「宗教」一詞從未出現；在《新約》中，它也只出現了三次；而且在其中一處經文中，譯文在「宗教」與「迷信」之間搖擺不定。在《使徒行傳》第二十六章第五節，我們讀到：「我按著我們宗教中最嚴謹的教門，作了法利賽人。」此處的「宗教」，在拉丁文《武加大譯本》中作「religio」，對應於希臘文「θρησκεία」，意指對諸神的外在崇拜。在《雅各書》（第一章第二十六至二十七節）中，我們見到「θρησκεία」（宗教崇拜）以及形容詞「θρῆσκος」，後者被譯為「religious」，在《武加大譯本》中則作「religiosus」。

在《加拉太書》（一13-14）中，使徒保羅提到他過去在猶太教中的熱忱，以及他如何極力迫害神的教會。然而，若我們要理解「宗教」一詞的真正起源，就必須追溯至拉丁文的 *religio*。這個詞的詞源學至今仍有爭議，但最常見的解釋來自西塞羅（Cicero），他將 *religio* 與 *relegere*（重新審視、細心考量）聯繫起來；另一種解釋則來自拉克坦提烏斯（Lactantius）與聖奧古斯丁（St. Augustine），他們將其與 *religare*（重新綁縛、連結）相連，意指人與神之間的連結。無論如何，*religio* 在古典拉丁文中最初指涉的是一種對神聖事物的敬畏與謹慎態度，而非一套系統化的教義體系。

在英文中，「猶太人的宗教」一詞旨在翻譯希臘文「Ἰουδαϊσμός」，此詞在拉丁文《武加大譯本》中保留為「Judaismus」。最後，在《使徒行傳》第廿五章第十九節中：「他們有幾件控告他的事，是關於他們自己的迷信，以及一個名叫耶穌、已經死了、保羅卻說他還活著的人」，希臘文原文為「δεισιδαιμονία」，此詞原意為對神靈的敬畏，《武加大譯本》正確地譯為「superstitio」，而修訂版則較不準確地譯為「religion」。在以上所有經文中，《武加大譯本》所使用的「religio」一詞，所指涉的是一套宗教信仰與崇拜體系；這已不再是「religio」在其古典意義上的內涵。最接近「religio」原始意義的希臘文詞彙是「εὐσέβεια」。動詞「σέβομαι」最初表達的是感到敬畏、因敬畏而退避。因此，「σέβας μ' ἔχει εἰσορῶντα」意為「當我注視時，敬畏攫住了我」。此詞後來用於表達對神靈的崇敬。因此，索福克勒斯在《伊蕾克特拉》（第1097行）中使用「εὐσέβεια Ζηνός」來表達對宙斯的崇敬；而同一詞彙加上介詞「εἰς」時，則出現於對父母的虔敬之意，例如柏拉圖《理想國》615 C 中的「εὐσέβεια εἰς θεοὺς καὶ γονέας」。在荷馬之後，我們發現「σέβομαι」可接直接受格，如同拉丁文「veneror」，例如「σέβομαι θεούς」，意為「我敬拜諸神」。
起初，希臘人使用 δεισιδαιμονία（deisidaimonia）來表達對神靈的畏懼。¹ ¹ 聖經中其他關於宗教的用語包括 φόβος τοῦ Θεοῦ（對上帝的敬畏）、λατρεία（禮拜）、δουλεία（服事）。參見 E. Hatch，《聖經希臘文論文集》（Essays in Biblical Greek），第 45 頁。

2 Brugmann 將 ἀτιμάω 與 *θέμις 溯源至梵語 *tyag*（意為「離開」）的推論，因詞義差異而難以成立；參見 Kuhn 的《期刊》第 25 卷，第 301 頁。若必須提出詞源解釋，我傾向將 *θέμις* 與 *σοβέω*（意為「驅趕」）及梵語 *kshubh*（意為「擾亂」）聯繫。希臘語中 *ks* 轉為 *s* 雖屬不規則變化，但非無例可循；參見 Curtius 著作第 696 頁。在 *kshubh* 中，我們或需承認其為 *ksabh* 的平行形式——然此說極不確定。
諸神或惡魔，以及 *φοβεῖσθαι τὸ θεῖον*（敬畏神聖力量），具有正面含義。但很快地，*δεισιδαιμονία* 便被用於負面意義，指涉迷信；因此，馬可‧奧理略‧安東尼努斯（Marcus Aurelius Antoninus，西元 161–180 年）提到 *θεοσεβὴς χωρὶς δεισιδαιμονίας*，即「不帶迷信的敬畏神」¹。
宗教學的起點，並非任何教義定義，而是歷史事實。我們必須先問：「迄今為止，人類實際上稱之為宗教的是什麼？」而非：「宗教應該是什麼？」若我們從一個預設的定義出發，便會冒著將整個宗教學領域縮減為僅是我們自身宗教觀點之反映的風險。真正的宗教學，如同語言科學，必須從最廣泛的比較開始，從所有已知宗教形式中蒐集事實，然後才試圖歸納出宗教本質的普遍法則。

現在我們要探討第三類定義，我稱之為獨斷式。它們有別於語源學定義與歷史定義，因為它們呈現的是個人的見解；無論是神學家或哲學家，他們自行其是地斷言，與其說宗教**曾經**或**現在**意指什麼，不如說宗教**應當**意指什麼。這類定義通常帶有某種專斷的語氣。我翻開一本哲學期刊¹，便接連看到以下關於宗教的定義：「宗教是我們對自然統一體的認識，教導我們將自身視為整體的一部分；誰能懷疑它對我們一切行為的強大影響力！」在下一頁，我又讀到：「神學與形上學與道德毫無關聯。」緊接著又說：「宗教從來就只是科學，再加上崇拜或情感。」 --- ¹ 此處原文保留數字註腳，譯文亦保留其形式。
我們幾乎翻開任何一本書，都會遇到這類隨意定義宗教的說法。有人說宗教是知識，也有人說宗教是無知；有人說宗教是自由，也有人說宗教是依賴；有人說宗教是慾望，也有人說宗教是脫離一切慾望；有人說宗教是靜默的沉思，也有人說宗教是輝煌的*行動*¹。 --- ¹ 原文此處為希臘文夾注，保留不譯。

> 「宗教學」（the science of religion）這個名稱，在過去幾年間已逐漸為人所接受，用以指稱那門以歷史與比較方法研究宗教現象的學科。它與「比較神學」（comparative theology）或「比較神話學」（comparative mythology）不同，後兩者僅處理宗教的某一部分，而宗教學則試圖涵蓋宗教的全部領域。正如語言科學（the science of language）將所有語言——無論是雅利安語（Aryan）、閃語族（Semitic）或圖蘭語族（Turanian）——視為同一種人類官能的表現，宗教學也將所有宗教——無論是吠陀（Veda）的宗教、奧義書（Upanishad）的宗教，還是其他任何宗教——視為同一種人類本能的展現。這種本能，即對無限者（the Infinite）的感知，在人類歷史中表現為多種形式：從最粗糙的多神教（polytheism），到單一神教（henotheism）與輪換主神教（kathenotheism），再到最高的一神教（monotheism）。在梨俱吠陀（Rig-Veda）中，我們看到特尤斯（Dyaus）與提婆（Deva）等名稱，這些詞彙最初或許僅指「光明」或「天空」，卻逐漸被賦予神聖的意涵。穆勒（Müller）曾將神話的起源描述為「語言的疾病」（a disease of language），這並非貶抑，而是指出語言在傳遞關於無限者的直觀時，必然會產生的扭曲與隱喻。因此，宗教學的任務，便是追溯這些隱喻的根源，並在歷史的層層積累中，還原人類對無限者的原始直觀。」

對上帝的莊嚴崇拜。人們對這個古老的詞語任意賦予各種意義。年輕詩人會告訴你，詩歌就是他們的宗教；年輕藝術家會說，他們的宗教就是藝術；而古語早已有云：「純潔的宗教，就是看顧患難中的孤兒寡婦，並且保守自己不沾染世俗。」我們無法否認每個人都有權按照自己的理解來定義宗教。請看定義問題的實際狀況：我們有宗教的語源學意義，但那並非具有約束力；我們也有宗教的各種歷史意義，而這些同樣不具約束力。那麼，我們能發現什麼標準，來檢驗我所謂的宗教教條式定義的真偽呢？有些定義顯然過於狹隘，有些則過於寬泛；有些本身存在謬誤，有些在應用於歷史事實時則顯得不夠充分。我們必須檢視其中最重要的定義——即便只檢視最重要的那些，無疑也會耗費不少時間——但我們應當記得，柏拉圖曾多次將整篇對話錄奉獻給這類哲學性的偵察工作，因此不應吝惜我們必須投入這項初步探究的時間。

The science of religion, as we have defined it, is totally distinct from theology. 宗教學，正如我們所定義的，與神學截然不同。 Theology, in the true sense of the word, is the science of God, or, if I may say so, the science of the divine; 神學，就其字面真義而言，是關於上帝的科學，或者，容我這麼說，是關於神聖者的科學； the science of religion is the science of the religious phenomena of the human mind, 而宗教學則是關於人類心靈之宗教現象的科學， and it is as independent of theology as the science of language is of grammar, or the science of thought of logic. 它獨立於神學，正如語言科學獨立於文法，或思維科學獨立於邏輯。
在進行這項探究時，我們必須謹慎選擇與使用自身的詞語，並應盡可能嘗試讓每個詞語僅表達單一意義。舉例而言，我們必須區分宗教（religion）與神學（theology），儘管這兩個詞經常被混用¹。 ¹ 雅各書第一章第27節。
45 一直混用。我們應始終以「宗教」指稱該主題本身，以「神學」指稱對該主題的研究或科學。就「神學」一詞而言，此術語自阿伯拉爾（Abelard）時代以來便已通行，似乎沒有理由加以更改。

希臘文 **th-eologos** 一詞最初用於不同的意義。例如，荷馬與赫西俄德被稱為 **theologi**（希羅多德，ii. 53），這並非現代意義上的神學家，而是指他們通曉諸神的起源與歷史。赫西俄德的《神譜》本可稱為他的神學，或至少是其一部分；而這個名稱也被用於類似作品，如塔米里斯的神學，以及奧菲斯的神學——後者尤其被新柏拉圖主義者稱為 **ὁ θεολόγος**（那位神學家）¹。柏拉圖與亞里斯多德則將神學用於「關於神性與神聖事物的學說」之意，即 **λόγοι περὶ τοῦ θεοῦ καὶ περὶ τῶν θείων**（關於神以及神聖事物的論述）。
在拉丁文中，**theologia** 被瓦羅（Varro）用來指稱我們所謂的宗教，依他之見，神學有三種：神話神學、物理神學與公民神學。神話神學包含關於諸神的寓言，據說其中許多內容有違不朽者的尊嚴。物理神學被他描述為超出凡俗之人的理解能力，而他則認為公民神學——即羅馬所接受的宗教——最適合一位好公民去信奉。
在基督教用語中，theologos 首先以《啟示錄》作者之名出現，即約翰神學家（John the Divine），或稱 theologos。然而，據我們所知，這個稱號之所以賦予他，並非僅僅因為他……
我們所謂的神學家，乃是因為他堅持道（Logos）的神性。在三、四世紀時，*theologos* 據說通常指捍衛該教義的人。
後來，特別是在中世紀時期，神學（theology）逐漸意指一般性的宗教教義，由神學家或祭司所研究；而亞伯拉德（Abelard）的《基督教神學》（Theologia Christiana）旨在呈現後來被稱為《神學大全》（Summa theologiae）的內容，即一套關於基督教宗教的系統化知識體系¹。
Dogmatic and Practical Religion.

曾有一段時間流行的做法，特別是在德國，將「宗教」一詞專指實踐與道德層面的宗教，而保留「神學」作為教義宗教的名稱，這種做法實有可議之處，只會造成混淆。我們固然可以區分教義宗教與實踐宗教，同樣也可以區分教義神學與實踐神學。然而，既然「神學家」一詞如今總是用來指稱以宗教為專業研究者，或隸屬於神學院教職者，那麼最好還是將「神學」保留為這門學問的名稱。僅僅信仰某個宗教教義的人，尚不能稱為神學家。因此，我提議保留「宗教」一詞的普遍意義，涵蓋教義宗教與實踐宗教兩者，而將「神學」保留作為對這兩者進行科學研究的名稱。如此一來，便能避免一切誤解，除非我們寧可完全棄用「神學」一詞，並以「宗教學」之名取而代之。
1 見弗林特（Flint），《大英百科全書》，「神學」條目。

因此，比較神學（comparative theology）——這個名稱或許比「宗教學」（the science of religion）更不易招致反對——的目標，在於揭示宗教現象的條件，並追溯其歷史發展。然而，在我們能夠著手這項工作之前，我們必須先對宗教本身有一個清晰的定義。我們必須知道我們所尋找的是什麼，否則我們可能會在尋找的過程中迷失方向。我們必須為自己釐清，宗教究竟是什麼，以及它與其他心靈活動——如哲學、倫理學，甚至神話——的區別何在。因為，正如我們所見，宗教與神話之間的界線往往模糊不清，而將兩者混淆，正是宗教研究中許多最嚴重錯誤的根源。
同樣地，所謂「比較宗教學」也只是對術語的濫用。存在宗教，也存在宗教學，正如存在語言與語言科學一般。但沒有人會說「比較語言學」；我們也不該說「比較宗教學」。神話學則不然。神話學一詞不僅可用於指稱神話的彙集，也可指對神話的科學研究；因此，在後者的意義上，說「比較神話學」是正確的。
我們迄今區分了：教義與實踐的宗教，以及教義與實踐的神學。

對某些哲學家與神學家而言，將宗教劃分為實踐性與教義性兩類似乎令人反感，甚至不可能成立，因為他們主張：若缺乏對神聖秩序——或至少對理性治理世界——的某種信念，道德便不可能存在；而教義若未能成為實踐道德的動機，亦將形同虛設。此說或許屬實，但我們目前無須深入探討此問題；因為僅以「教義性」或「實踐性」來限定宗教，我們只是在區分，而非分離。在尚未對「道德能否脫離教義而存在」或「教義能否脫離道德而成立」等問題預設立場之前，我們透過此種命名法所承認的，不過是兩者之間存在某種共通元素罷了。

> Religion is the feeling of absolute dependence; that is, the consciousness that we are, through and through, dependent on something that is not ourselves, and that is the Infinite. 宗教是絕對依賴的感受；也就是說，我們徹頭徹尾依賴於某個非我之物、即無限者的意識。
然而，有些哲學家，特別是施萊爾馬赫（Schleiermacher），主張有權以一種更為……的方式使用「宗教」一詞。

更高層次的意義。他們否認宗教是教條式的或道德性的；他們也否認教條與道德的結合能構成宗教。他們指出，當我們說某人沒有宗教信仰時，並非單純指他不信奉猶太教、基督教或任何其他信仰形式，或拒絕服從其道德規範。我們真正的意思是，他缺乏任何宗教情感。施萊爾馬赫將宗教情感解釋為一種直接的意識：一切看似有限者皆是無限，一切看似短暫者皆是永恆。「在一切生命與運動、一切變遷與機遇、一切作為與受苦之中，去尋求並發現那無限與永恆，事實上，透過一種直接的情感去擁有並認識生命本身即為無限與永恆的生命，」他說，「這就是宗教。」——「從那個觀點出發，一旦達到此境界，所有事件都成為真實的奇蹟，所有奇蹟都成為真實的事件；所有經驗都成為啟示，所有啟示都成為經驗。」——「如果我們看不見自身周遭的奇蹟，如果我們感受不到內在的啟示，如果我們的靈魂不渴望汲取整個世界的美，並被其精神所滲透；如果在我們生命的最高時刻，我們未曾感受到被神聖精神所驅策，並從自身神聖的靈感中說話與行動，如果我們至少未曾將自己所感受的一切視為宇宙的直接影響，卻在其中發現某種屬於我們自身、無法模仿、卻能證明其純粹源頭存於我們內在之物，那麼我們便沒有宗教。」

我們在探討《奧義書》、吠檀多哲學、蘇菲詩歌以及中世紀神秘主義者的思辨時，將必須考量宗教的這種意涵；然而在我看來，若能為宗教可能達到的最高境界另立一名，或許更為妥當——因為與其說那是一套關於神聖者的教義體系，或一套受我們對神聖者之信仰所啟發的誡律規範，不如說它更像是人性的徹底轉化。德文稱之為 *Religiosität*；英文則可用 *religiousness* 或 *devotion* 來表達相同的意思。
宗教，無論是信仰抑或教義體系。

我們在探討必須檢視的宗教之重大歷史定義之前，仍須對「宗教」一詞的日常用法作一補充說明，方能確保自身具備恰當的準備。如同許多同類術語，「宗教」既可指我們自身對理論教義與道德原則的智識掌握，亦可指由權威彙集而成、主要用於教導這些教義與實踐的一套學說與規條。因此，我們可以說某人從猶太教改信基督教，亦即他改變了自身的宗教信念；但我們也可以說某人正在研究佛教，無論是透過閱讀佛教聖典，或是在錫蘭或中國觀察佛教徒的生活，而這些研究對其自身信念絲毫不產生影響。這種歧義幾乎無可避免，我們必須在所有知識領域中對此有所考量。我們談論邏輯時，既可指思維的規律，亦可指關於思維規律的科學。
我們自己是否認識並遵循它們，或者是否有一套包含在論文與手冊中的教義體系；而當我們談論宗教時，也必須牢記同樣的雙重意涵。
我希望，嚴格遵循我們現已闡明的術語，將有助於我們避免許多誤解，同時使我們能夠將各種宗教定義歸入其恰當的位置。
